nativeRange.habitat: Found in shallow tropical reef environments including reef slopes and lagoons, where colonies encrust over rock and rubble substrate.

decorPreferences: open placement with spacing from neighbors
stable base for frag plug or rock

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via symbiotic zooxanthellae. Supplemental target feeding of small zooplankton or phytoplankton is not generally required, though colonies in low-nutrient systems may benefit from occasional additions of dissolved organic matter or micro-plankton.

goodWith: other soft corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: aggressive LPS with long sweeper tentacles
aggressive encrusting corals that can overgrow them

imageCandidates.notes: PilarMeca, 3888x2592. Clavularia (Pachyclavularia) viridis, the Green Star Polyp. Sharp in-water shot of vivid fluorescent-green eight-tentacled polyps against a dark background - the classic reef look hobbyists buy.
Bernard DUPONT, 4416x2649. Clavularia sp. in situ; crisp view of individual eight-armed star polyps with bright green rays and white centers - excellent diagnostic of the genus.
Bernard DUPONT, 4416x2649. Clavularia sp.; sharp close view showing the feathery pinnate eight-tentacle structure of many polyps in a colony.
User:Haplochromis, 1536x1440. Clavularia (Pachyclavularia) viridis encrusting rock with teal-green polyps - shows the mat-forming growth habit.
Samuel Chow, 1600x1200. Clavularia sp. in situ at Sipadan; clear white/tan eight-tentacled star polyps - adds a color/species variant to the genus set.
Luca Davenport-Thomas, 2048x1536. Clavularia novaezealandiae - a distinct temperate species with brown pinnate polyps; broadens the genus representation.

summary: The Jasmine Polyp Rock, also sold as Clove or Glove Polyp, is a soft octocoral (Clavularia sp.) that spreads across rock surfaces in colonies of distinctive eight-tentacled polyps. It is widely considered one of the more beginner-friendly corals, thriving under low-to-moderate light and gentle flow.
careNotes:
The Jasmine Polyp Rock is sold at genus level in the aquarium trade because care requirements are broadly consistent across the roughly 69 accepted species of Clavularia. Aquarium specimens are typically Indo-Pacific in origin, most often sourced from Indonesia or Australia.

Lighting: Low-to-moderate intensity is generally advised, with most recommendations falling in the range of 50–150 PAR. These polyps tend to close under excessively bright light and have been reported thriving under standard T5, LED, or PC fixtures at moderate output. Placement depth can be adjusted to fine-tune the light level the colony receives.

Flow: Low-to-moderate, indirect flow is generally preferred. Direct laminar flow tends to cause the polyps to retract; gentle, randomized current allows them to extend fully and makes dissolved nutrients easier to capture.

Placement: As an encrusting coral, Clavularia can be placed essentially anywhere in the aquarium — on the bottom, along the middle rockwork, or higher up. Frags placed on live rock or frag plugs will spread across available substrate over time. Spacing from neighboring corals is important, since colonies can overgrow adjacent species.

Aggression: Clavularia does not possess the long sweeper tentacles found on many LPS corals (large-polyp stony corals) and tends to be peaceful in terms of direct physical contact. Like many soft corals, however, it may engage in chemical competition — known as allelopathy — with nearby corals, particularly when colonies are placed in close proximity. Jasmine Polyp Rock is also susceptible to stings from more aggressive LPS neighbors.

Feeding: The colony is primarily photosynthetic, drawing energy through symbiotic algae (zooxanthellae) living in its tissue. Supplemental direct feeding is not typically required; healthy colonies in reef systems with normal biological activity generally receive adequate dissolved nutrients. Some hobbyists have reported improved polyp extension when small amounts of zooplankton are added to the water column.

Growth: The colony tends to encrust rapidly in established reef aquariums under good conditions. A minimum tank volume of 76 liters (20 gallons) is commonly cited, though the main practical constraint is stable reef chemistry rather than physical space.
breedingNotes: Propagation by fragging is generally considered straightforward and is widely practiced by reef hobbyists. Colonies can be divided by cutting or breaking off sections of encrusted rock or frag plug; the fragments tend to reattach readily and continue spreading. Sexual reproduction occurs via broadcast spawning in the wild, but is not commonly attempted or achieved in captivity.

additional.name: World Wide Corals – Clove Polyps (multiple listings)
additional.notes: Cross-checked care level (Easy), lighting (Medium), flow (Low), and temperature (72–76°F). WWC rates care as Easy vs. LiveAquaria Moderate; beginner adopted as consensus. Temperature averaged: LiveAquaria 72–78°F vs. WWC 72–76°F → 22–25°C adopted. WWC product pages lack detailed water chemistry parameters (salinity, pH, dKH, Ca, Mg) — LiveAquaria values used for those.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Clavularia (taxonomy)
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy only: confirms family Clavulariidae (not Xeniidae as listed on LiveAquaria), order Malacalcyonacea, genus established by Blainville 1830. Also confirms common names star polyps and clove polyps, and ~69 accepted species with worldwide marine distribution including Indo-Pacific tropical species.
